sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 2, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 1 of about 39 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after the DOWN action. I need to compare the previous frame to the current frame to see if the blue object moved.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed after DOWN
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = current_frame.segmentation

# Find blue node in both frames
def find_node(seg, color):
    for n in seg['nodes']:
        if n['color'] == color:
            return n
    return None

blue_before = find_node(before, 'b')
blue_after = find_node(after, 'b')

print(f"Blue before: boundary={blue_before['boundary'] if blue_before else 'None'}")
print(f"Blue after:  boundary={blue_after['boundary'] if blue_after else 'None'}")

# Check all nodes for changes
print("\nBefore nodes:")
for n in before['nodes']:
    print(f"  {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("\nAfter nodes:")
for n in after['nodes']:
    print(f"  {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Testing - need to see if blue object moves"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N_strip_top": "Green timer/HUD bar at row 0", "O_background": "Orange main background", "c_block": "Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39", "M_block": "Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39", "b_rectangle": "Blue 4x20 rectangle at rows 16-19, cols 12-31 - likely player", "Y_shapes": "Two yellow 2x10 rectangles at bottom rows 58-59", "w_strip_bottom": "Light gray floor rows 60-63"}, "hud_map": {"top_green_strip": "Timer or progress bar", "bottom_gray_strip": "Floor"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Blue rectangle is the player. Two yellow blocks at bottom. Charcoal+magenta blocks near top center. Need to understand goal.", "goal_model": "Unknown - possibly collect items or reach a target", "recent_findings": ["DOWN action executed, checking if blue object moved"], "open_questions": ["Does DOWN move the blue object?", "What is the goal?"], "current_plan": "Check if movement works, then explore all directions", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key findings:
1. The blue object moved DOWN by 4 rows (from rows 16-19 to rows 20-23). So DOWN moves the player 4 rows down.
2. The green (N) strip at top shrunk from 64 pixels to 62 pixels - it lost 2 pixels. This confirms it's a timer/progress bar that decreases with each action.
3. A new white (W) node appeared at the top right (2 pixels) - this might be the timer showing remaining moves.
So the game mechanics are:
- Arrow keys move the blue player rectangle
- The green bar at top is a timer that decreases with each move
- Goal is likely to reach something before the timer runs out
The blue player is at rows 20-23, cols 12-31. The yellow blocks are at rows 52-59 area. Let me try to navigate toward them. But first, let me understand the movement better - DOWN moved 4 rows. Let me check if the player can move in other directions too.
Actually, let me think about the goal. The yellow shapes at the bottom could be platforms to stand on, or collectibles. The charcoal and magenta blocks near the top might be obstacles or targets.
Given the timer is decreasing, I should try to figure out the goal quickly. Let me try moving toward one of the yellow blocks to see what happens. But the yellow blocks are quite far down. Let me try a few more moves to understand the movement pattern better and potentially reach the yellow blocks.
The blue object is 4 rows tall and 20 columns wide. It moved from rows 16-19 to 20-23 (4 rows down). Let me continue moving down to reach the yellow blocks.
Actually, let me be more efficient. The yellow blocks are at rows 52-59, and the player is now at rows 20-23. That's about 30 rows away. At 4 rows per DOWN action, that's about 8 more DOWN actions. But the timer is decreasing by 2 per action, so I have about 31 more actions (62/2).
Let me try moving down several times to reach the yellow blocks.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue player moved from rows 16-19 to 20-23 (4 rows down per DOWN action)
# Timer (green bar) decreased from 64 to 62 pixels (-2 per action)
# Yellow blocks are at rows 52-59, player at 20-23
# Need ~8 more DOWN to reach yellow blocks
# Let me batch several DOWN moves to get close to the yellow blocks

action(['DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"DOWN": "Moves blue player 4 rows down, decreases timer by 2 pixels VERIFIED(step 2)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"N_strip_top": "Green timer bar at row 0, shrinks by 2 per action", "O_background": "Orange main background", "c_block": "Charcoal block at rows 1-3, cols 36-39", "M_block": "Magenta block at rows 4-7, cols 36-39", "b_rectangle": "Blue 4x20 player rectangle, moves with arrow keys", "Y_shapes": "Two yellow shapes at bottom rows 52-59", "w_strip_bottom": "Light gray floor rows 60-63"}, "hud_map": {"top_green_strip": "Timer/progress bar"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Blue 4x20 rectangle is player, moves 4 pixels per direction. Green timer bar decreases 2 pixels per action. Two yellow blocks at bottom. Charcoal+magenta blocks near top center.", "goal_model": "Likely reach or interact with yellow blocks, or charcoal/magenta blocks", "recent_findings": ["DOWN moves player 4 rows down", "Timer decreases 2 pixels per action"], "open_questions": ["What happens when reaching yellow blocks?", "What is the exact goal?"], "current_plan": "Move down toward yellow blocks to investigate", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": []}}
